coredata.dc:title: Stimuli-responsive carbon nanotubes based on the interlinkage of carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Huiting
Xie, Xiaoyan
Lin, Dongxue
Zhou, Jie
Zhang, Ling
Xing, Zhiqiang
Zhang, Qian
Xia, Lixin
coredata.dc:description: The preparation of stimuli-responsive carbon nanotubes (CNTs) is often achieved through covalent connection between stimuli-responsive polymers and CNTs. However, pre-treatment of CNTs to achieve covalent bonding can disrupt the surface structure of CNTs, so as to reduce the electron delocalization of CNTs, thereby damaging its inherent optical and electronic properties. In this work, a simple and non-destructive method was demonstrated to construct various stimulus responsive CNTs by using a carbonous material, carbon dots (CDs), as joint part. CDs with large π bond can interact with non-polar carbon nanotubes (CNTs) through π-π interaction without causing damage to the surface of CNTs. The epoxy bond of modified CDs provides binding sites for polymer initiators 2,2′-azobis[2-methylpropionamide] dihydrochloride (AIBA). Based on this, stimuli-responsive polymers such as poly (N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAm) or polyacrylic acid (PAA) can be loaded onto the CDs modified CNTs, thereby achieving the construction of stimulus responsive CNTs. When the temperature or pH switches, the surface water contact angle (WCAs) of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm varies between high and low values because of the shrinkage and extension of PNIPAm and PAA chains. Such unique property can also lead to the utilization of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm as an intelligent electrode material. The changes in the surface hydrophobicity of materials can regulate the contact of water-soluble electrochemical probes with modified electrode surfaces, thereby controlling the expression of electrochemical activity. The prepared modified electrodes exhibit reversible ’ON-OFF’ switching effect against temperature or pH changes. This non-destructive method for constructing stimuli-responsive CNTs holds great potential for applications in the field of intelligent electrochemical sensors and devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Nanotubes
Stimuli-Responsive
Carbon Dots
‘ON-OFF’ Switching

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs (a) and CNTs/CDs (b) (Inset: digital image of CDs solution (Ⅰ), CNTs/CDs (Ⅱ) irradiated by UV Lamp). (B) XPS spectra of CNTs/CDs (a) and CNTs/CDs-AIBA (b) (Inset:C1s XPS spectra of CNTs/CDs (Ⅰ) and CNTs/CDs-AIBA (Ⅱ)). (C) FT-IR spectra of CNTs (a) and CNTs/CDs (b), CNTs/CDs-PAA (c), CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm (d) and CDs (e). (D) XPS spectra of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm (Inset:C1s XPS spectra of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm). (E) Digital images of dispersion (1 mg/mL) of CNTs/PNIPAm (Ⅰ), CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm (Ⅱ), CNTs/PAA (Ⅲ) and CNTs/CDs-PAA (Ⅳ) at 20 °C, pH = 10.

Type Ⅰ − polymers/CNTs: Digital image of CNTs/PNIPAm at 20 °C (A) and 40 °C (B); TEM images of CNTs/PNIPAm (C) and CNTs/CDs-PAA (D); Digital image of CNTs/PAA at pH = 3 (E) and pH = 10 (F); Type Ⅱ − mixture of CNTs/CDs and polymers: Digital image of mixture of CNTs/CDs and free PNIPAm at 20 °C (G) and 40 °C (H); Type Ⅲ − mixture of CNTs and CDs-polymers: Digital image of mixture of CNTs and CDs-PNIPAm at 20 °C (I) and 40 °C (J); Type Ⅳ − CNTs/CDs-polymers: Digital image of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm at 20 °C (K) and 40 °C (L); TEM images of CNTs (M), CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm (N); Digital image of CNTs/CDs-PAA at pH = 3 (O) and pH = 10 (P).

Water contact angles of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm at 20 °C (A) and 40 °C (B); Water contact angles of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm heating–cooling cycles between 20 °C and 40 °C (C). Water contact angles of CNTs/CDs-PAA at pH = 3 (D) and pH = 10 (E); Water contact angles of CNTs/CDs-PAA aciding–alkalising cycles between pH = 3 (red points) and pH = 10 (blue points) (F).

(A) Schematic of the ‘ON-OFF’ switching effect of electrochemical signals. (B) Cyclic voltammograms of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm/GC modified electrodes in 5 mM K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 (1:1) containing 0.5 M KCl, scan rate of 200 mV s−1 at 20 °C and 40 °C. (C) The current changes of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm/GC electrode upon temperature variation.
(A) Schematic of the modification of AIBA on CDs by ring opening reaction; (B) Schematic of the construction of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm and CNTs/CDs-PAA and their stimuli-responsiveness.

head.ce:title: Stimuli-responsive carbon nanotubes based on the interlinkage of carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shenyang Normal University, Shenyang 110034, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The preparation of stimuli-responsive carbon nanotubes (CNTs) is often achieved through covalent connection between stimuli-responsive polymers and CNTs. However, pre-treatment of CNTs to achieve covalent bonding can disrupt the surface structure of CNTs, so as to reduce the electron delocalization of CNTs, thereby damaging its inherent optical and electronic properties. In this work, a simple and non-destructive method was demonstrated to construct various stimulus responsive CNTs by using a carbonous material, carbon dots (CDs), as joint part. CDs with large π bond can interact with non-polar carbon nanotubes (CNTs) through π-π interaction without causing damage to the surface of CNTs. The epoxy bond of modified CDs provides binding sites for polymer initiators 2,2′-azobis[2-methylpropionamide] dihydrochloride (AIBA). Based on this, stimuli-responsive polymers such as poly (N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAm) or polyacrylic acid (PAA) can be loaded onto the CDs modified CNTs, thereby achieving the construction of stimulus responsive CNTs. When the temperature or pH switches, the surface water contact angle (WCAs) of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm varies between high and low values because of the shrinkage and extension of PNIPAm and PAA chains. Such unique property can also lead to the utilization of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm as an intelligent electrode material. The changes in the surface hydrophobicity of materials can regulate the contact of water-soluble electrochemical probes with modified electrode surfaces, thereby controlling the expression of electrochemical activity. The prepared modified electrodes exhibit reversible ’ON-OFF’ switching effect against temperature or pH changes. This non-destructive method for constructing stimuli-responsive CNTs holds great potential for applications in the field of intelligent electrochemical sensors and devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Stimuli-responsive carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have extraordinarily value in many applications ranging from mechanical, electrical, electrochemistry and sensing because of their unique stimuli-responsiveness and structure [1–7]. Covalent modification has been proven to be an effective way to connect CNTs and polymers with stimuli-responsiveness to construct stimuli-responsive CNTs. [8–10]. For example, PNIPAm has been combined with CNTs through a two-step approach. The surface of CNTs was firstly hydrophilicly treated using UV-ozone method, and PNIPAm can be subsequently in-situ polymerized on CNTs [11]. The method of acid solution treatment was also used to produce hydroxide groups on CNTs’ surface for the further grafting of PNIPAm [12]. Similar two-step methods have also been used for the binding of other polymers to CNTs. CNTs are first covalently modified by polydopamine (PDA) to obtain amino and hydroxide groups, thereby achieving the loading of poly (N-isopropyl acrylamide-co-glycidyl methylate) (PNG) chains on CNTs [13]. Although there are differences in the ways CNTs are modified, these covalent bonding methods all require surface pre-treatment of CNTs to introduce active sites due to the lack of reaction sites for polymer growth in unmodified CNTs. This pre-treatment often causes damage to the surface of CNTs. It was reported that the damage to the surface integrity of CNTs poses a significant threat to their performance [14–16]. The decrease in electronic decentralization caused by the destruction of surface structure can lead to damage to the intrinsic optical and electronic properties of CNTs, thereby limiting their applications in optoelectronic devices, sensing, and other fields. Therefore, developing a suitable non-covalent method to introduce surface active sites on CNTs can link stimuli-responsive polymers while maintain the excellent properties of CNTs.

Carbon dots (CDs), as typical water-soluble fluorescent nanomaterials, have non-polar surfaces and polar electron transport layers [17–20]. Therefore, it is highly possible for CDs to bind to components with different polarity. The large π bond of CDs can be combined with non-polar CNT through π-π interaction [21–24]. It is worth noting that such modification using carbonous materials is non-destructive and has no impact on the structural integrity of the CNTs’ surface. Meanwhile, in the event that CDs possess polar functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, the surface property of CNTs transition from non-polarity to polarity via the modification of CDs. These polar functional groups can endow CNTs with electric charge, resulting well dispersed CNTs due to electrostatic repulsion, thereby exhibiting potential for further modification. Besides that, epoxy bond of CDs can provide binding sites for the loading of polymer initiators, 2,2′-azobis [2-methylpropionamide] dihydrochloride (AIBA) [25] This allows different types of stimuli-responsive polymers, such as poly (N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAm) or polyacrylic acid (PAA), to be bonded to the modified CNTs. In other words, if CDs are used as surface modifiers for CNTs, it can achieve the implantation of active sites for polymerization reactions while maintaining the surface integrity of CNTs. It is worth noting that although other types of modifiers can also achieve the modification of CNTs with π-π interaction, the modifiers used such as small molecule and polymers often introduce structures that differ significantly from CNTs. Carbonbased materials CDs have a basic composition and structure similar to CNTs, which can maximize the consistency of CNTs structure during the modification process, which is beneficial for maintaining the inherent optical and electronic properties of CNTs. However, there has rarely relevant researches published about non-destructive construction of stimulus responsive CNTs using carbon based materials as linkers.

Here, we present a simple and non-destructive method to prepare stimuli-responsive polymer-CNTs composites with CDs as joint parts (Scheme 1 ). As shown, hydrophilic CDs were first modified onto hydrophobic CNTs by π − π interaction. This modification not only changes the surface hydrophobicity of CNTs, but also introduces sites for the binding of polymer initiator AIBA. Subsequently, AIBA was connected to CNTs/CDs through a ring opening reaction of epoxy bonds [26]. Upon activation, AIBA decomposes to form free radicals, which are initially generated on the surface of the CDs. These radicals play a critical role in initiating the polymerization process. Stimuli-responsive monomers such as N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAm) or acrylic acid (AA) are then added, and the surface-bound radicals initiate the polymerization, leading to the in-situ growth of poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAm) or polyacrylic acid (PAA) chains directly from the CDs modified on the CNTs. This approach results in the formation of stimulus-responsive CNTs. The as prepared modified CNTs is stimuli-responsive due to the sensitivity of PNIPAm against temperature and that of PAA against pH. Such responsiveness can also regulate the expression of electrochemical activity of stimuli-responsive CNTs modified electrodes, thereby exhibiting reversible ‘ON-OFF’ switching effect against temperature or pH changes.

The carbon nanotubes (CNTs) used in the experiment were multi-walled CNTs with a purity of over 70 %, purchased from Aladdin. The CNTs presented an averaged outside diameter of 30–60 nm, inside diameter of 20–50 nm, length of 1–10 μm. N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAm) (98 %) was purchased from Energy Chemical and recrystallized from hexane/acetone. 2,2′-azobis[2–methylpropionamidine] dihydrochloride (AIBA) (98 %) was purchased from Shanghai Kayon Biological Technol. Citric acid (C6H8O7) (AR) was purchased from Shenyang Reagent No. 1 Plant. Glycocoll (C2H5NO2) (AR), acroleic acid (AA) (98 %), muriate (KCl) (AR), potassium ferricyanide (K3Fe(CN)6) (AR), potassium ferrocyanide (K4Fe(CN)6) (AR) and n-hexane (C6H14) (AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd and AA were extracted from a 65 °C fraction by vacuum distillation for 3 h. Acetone (C3H6O), ammonia (NH3·H2O) (25 %) and concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from reagent company. All solutions were prepared using Milli-Q purified water (>18 MΩ cm−1) sterilized at high temperature.

TEM analysis was conducted using a JEOL JEM-2100 electron microscope operating at 120 kV. Samples were prepared by dispersing the material in ethanol, followed by drop-casting onto a copper grid coated with carbon film. The grids were dried at room temperature before imaging to ensure minimal solvent interference.

FT-IR spectra were recorded on a Perkin Elmer Spectrum One instrument using the KBr pellet method. Each sample was ground with KBr powder, pressed into a pellet, and scanned in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1. Background correction was performed to eliminate atmospheric interferences such as CO2 and water vapor.

UV–vis spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu UV-2100S spectrophotometer. Samples were measured in a quartz cuvette with an optical path length of 1 cm. The measurements were carried out across the 200–800 nm wavelength range to ensure the full absorption spectrum was captured. Baseline correction was applied using the appropriate solvent as a reference.

XPS analysis was performed using a LEO 1530 field-emission instrument with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV). The binding energy scale was calibrated against the C 1 s peak at 284.8 eV. All samples were dried and mounted on conductive adhesive tape prior to measurement. Spectral fitting was conducted using CasaXPS software to identify the chemical states.

Zeta potential measurements were performed using a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Panalytical, UK). Samples were dispersed in deionized water at a concentration of 1 mg/mL, and measurements were conducted at 25 °C. Each sample was measured three times to ensure consistency, and the average value was reported.

TGA was conducted using a SDTQ450 thermoanalyzer under a nitrogen atmosphere. Samples were heated from room temperature to 800 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min. The nitrogen flow rate was set at 50 mL/min to prevent oxidation. The weight loss data was analyzed to determine thermal stability and composition.

Molecular weights were measured using an Agilent 1260 GPC system equipped with a Waters Ultrahydrogel column (300 × 7.8 mm) and an Agilent RID G1362A detector. A 0.1 mol/L NaNO3 aqueous solution was used as the mobile phase, and PEG standards were employed for calibration. All measurements were conducted at a flow rate of 1 mL/min at 30 °C.

Contact angle measurements were performed using a Kruss DSA30S drop shape analyzer under ambient conditions (relative humidity of 35 ± 1 %). The water contact angle (WCA) was determined using the sessile drop method, with a droplet volume of 2 µL. The pellet for measuring the WCA was prepared by compressing the sample into uniform solid discs, providing a flat and even surface for the contact angle measurement. After each measurement, the pellet was dried and the process was repeated to ensure consistency. Three measurements were taken for each sample, and the average value was reported.

Raman spectra were collected using a Renishaw inVia Raman spectrometer with a 532 nm laser excitation source. Each sample was scanned three times for 10 s per scan with a laser power of 2.5 mW to prevent sample degradation. Baseline correction was applied to remove background noise.

Electrochemical measurements were conducted at room temperature using a CHI660 electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instruments, China). A three-electrode setup was used, with the modified electrode as the working electrode, an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, and a platinum wire as the counter electrode. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed using a 0.24 V DC bias with a frequency range from 100 kHz to 50 MHz, an alternating voltage of 10 mV was applied, and the data was fitted to an equivalent circuit model using ZsimpWin 3.0 software.

Emission spectra were recorded using an organic elemental analyzer (EA), specifically the Elementar UNICUBE model from Germany. The samples were excited at a specific wavelength, and the emission was monitored in the range of 300–700 nm. A slit width of 5 nm was used for both excitation and emission to ensure optimal resolution. Measurements were conducted in a quartz cuvette with an optical path length of 1 cm.

The synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) was carried out according to a modified version of the procedure reported in the literature [16]. Briefly, citric acid (5 g) and glycine (5 g) were used as the carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. The two precursors were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water in a 50 mL beaker and stirred until a homogeneous solution was obtained. The solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h to facilitate carbonization and nitrogen doping of the carbon dots. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting dark brown solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 30 min to remove larger particles and impurities. The supernatant was then filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane filter for further purification. The purified CDs were freeze-dried to obtain a dry powder, which was subsequently redispersed in deionized water to form a stable 10 mg/mL aqueous dispersion. The overall yield of the synthesis process was approximately 7.8 %, calculated based on the initial weight of the citric acid and glycine.

To prepare the CNTs/CDs composite, 0.5 g of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (CNTs, pretreated by refluxing in concentrated nitric acid to improve dispersibility) was added to 60 mL of the CD dispersion (10 mg/mL) in a 100 mL round-bottom flask. The mixture was first sonicated for 3 h using a probe sonicator (40 % amplitude) to ensure uniform dispersion and promote the adsorption of CDs onto the CNT surface. Following sonication, the mixture was stirred for an additional 3 h at room temperature. This sonication-stirring cycle was repeated three times to maximize the attachment of CDs onto the CNTs. The resulting dispersion was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to remove any unbound CDs, and the precipitate was washed thoroughly with deionized water to ensure the removal of excess CDs. Finally, the CNTs/CDs composite was dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h. The yield of the composite synthesis was approximately 83 %, calculated based on the initial weight of CNTs.

The preparation of the CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm composite was performed via free radical polymerization. First, 10 mL of the CNTs/CDs dispersion (10 mg/mL) was placed in a 100 mL round-bottom flask, along with 0.017 g of 2,2′-azobis(2-methylpropionamidine) dihydrochloride (AIBA) as the initiator. The pH of the mixture was adjusted to 9–10 using a 1 M aqueous ammonia solution to ensure optimal conditions for the polymerization reaction. Then, 1 g of recrystallized N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM) was added to the solution. The flask was sealed with a rubber septum and the mixture was degassed by bubbling nitrogen gas through the solution for 30 min to remove dissolved oxygen. The reaction temperature was initially set to 35 °C with constant stirring for 2 h to allow for partial polymerization. Subsequently, the temperature was increased to 80 °C, and the reaction was continued for an additional 3 h to complete the polymerization. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to separate the product from unreacted PNIPAm. The obtained CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm composite was washed with deionized water several times and dried under vacuum at 60 °C, resulting in a yield of approximately 88 %.

The synthesis of CNTs/CDs-PAA followed a similar protocol as for CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm. In a 100 mL round-bottom flask, 10 mL of the CNTs/CDs dispersion (10 mg/mL) was mixed with 0.017 g of AIBA and the pH was adjusted to 9–10 with 1 M ammonia solution. After degassing the solution with nitrogen gas for 30 min, the flask was heated to 35 °C and stirred for 2 h. Following this, 1 g of acrylic acid (AA) was introduced into the flask. The temperature was then raised to 80 °C, and the reaction was allowed to proceed for 3 more hours. To purify the product, the resulting mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove excess poly(acrylic acid). The purified CNTs/CDs-PAA composite was obtained after several washings with deionized water and vacuum drying at 60 °C, with a final yield of approximately 86 %.

Glassy carbon (GC) electrodes (3 mm diameter) were polished sequentially using 1.0, 0.3, and 0.05 μm alumina slurry on a polishing cloth, followed by thorough rinsing with deionized water. The polished electrodes were then sonicated in deionized water for 5 min to remove any residual polishing compounds and subsequently dried using high-purity nitrogen gas. The PNIPAm/GC, CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm/GC, PAA/GC, and CNTs/CDs-PAA/GC electrodes were fabricated via a drop-casting method. For each electrode, 7 μL of the corresponding aqueous dispersion (10 mg/mL) was carefully pipetted onto the GC electrode surface. The electrode was then covered with an inverted beaker to minimize dust contamination and allowed to dry slowly at room temperature for at least 12 h to ensure the formation of a uniform thin film. This procedure was consistently applied for all sample types to ensure comparable film thickness and electrode coverage. The fabricated electrodes were stored in a desiccator prior to use in electrochemical measurements.

For the temperature-responsive experiments, the solution used was a phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1 M, pH 7). The temperature of the solution was adjusted using a water bath with precise temperature control. The temperature was varied between 20 °C and 40 °C, and the system was allowed to equilibrate at each temperature for 10 min before measurements were taken. This ensured that the stimuli-responsive behavior of the composite and modified electrodes could be observed across a range of temperatures.

For the pH-responsive experiments, the responsiveness of the system was tested in PBS solutions at different pH levels. PBS (0.1 M) was prepared at pH 3, and pH 10 by adjusting the pH of the buffer with 0.1 M hydrochloric acid (HCl) for acidic conditions or 0.1 M sodium hydroxide (NaOH) for basic conditions. After measurements at each pH level, the electrode was carefully rinsed with deionized water to remove any remaining buffer solution and then immersed in the next PBS solution of the required pH. The system was allowed to equilibrate for 10 min in the new buffer solution before further measurements were conducted.

The combination of CDs and CNTs were confirmed by fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1
A). As can be seen in Fig. 1 A, before combined with CNTs, CDs emitted strong fluorescence with peak centered at 448 nm under single-wavelength excitation. It can be observed directly from the Inset Ⅰ that a blue light emitted from CDs under the irradiation of UV lamp. Significantly, the blue fluorescence of CDs disappeared after the combination of CNTs (Inset Ⅱ), accompanied by the disappearance of the fluorescence peak (curve b). The possible quenching mechanism was that the interaction between CNTs and CDs leads to the transfer of electrons from CDs to CNTs, which can act as electron receptors under photoexcitation [27]. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the CDs and CDs/CNTs was provided. As can be seen in Figure S1 , the mean size of CDs is 3 nm. As shown in Figure S2
B, the CDs appear as well-dispersed, distinct particles on the surface of the CNTs, confirming their effective attachment.

The elemental composition of CNTs/CDs was characterized by Xray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. As shown in Fig. 1 B, the expected O 1 s signal was flanked by peak at 532.5 eV, related to oxygen containing group of CDs. Furthermore, the XPS C 1 s core-level spectrum of CNTs/CDs (Fig. 1 B, inset Ⅰ) can be curve-fitted into four peaks with binding energies at about 283.5 eV, 284.28 eV, 285.27 eV and 290.2 eV, respectively. The appearance of these peaks attributed to the sp2 hybridized carbon, sp3 hybridized carbon, and the carbon elements in O = C-O and C-N bonds, clearly illustrates the presence of CDs on the surface of CNTs.

The modification of AIBA triggers the in-situ growth of stimuli-responsive polymers. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of CDs and CDs-AIBA were shown in Figure S3 . It can be observed in the both curves of CDs and CDs-AIBA that the adsorption bands located at about 1720, 1400 and 1080 cm−1 were attributed to carbonyl group (C = O) in the carboxylic (COOH) group, strong stretching and vibration peak of C-O of CDs, respectively. In addition, in curve a, the characteristic peak at about 1330 cm−1 was assigned to the C-O-C from the epoxy groups of CDs. By contrast, it is clearly shown in FT-IR curve of CDs-AIBA that the obvious new characteristic absorption bands appeared at 1570 cm−1, which is attributed to the bending vibration of C-N of CDs-AIBA, indicating that a covalently bond was successfully forming between CDs and AIBA. Meanwhile, the characteristic peak at 1330 cm−1 was disappeared on curve b, demonstrating the disappearance of epoxy groups, further indicating that the binding of CDs and AIBA was achieved through ring opening reaction.

The FT-IR spectroscopy was also performed to monitor the subsequent synthesis process of CNTs/CDs-PAA and CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm (Fig. 1 C). Take CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm as an example, the broad band at 3000–3500 cm−1 is indicative of stretching vibration of O-H attributed to water molecules hydrogen-bonded to the amide groups of PNIPAm. The sharp peak at 1651 cm−1 is attributed to the C = O stretching vibration of secondary amide in PNIPAm, indicating the successful combination of PNIPAm onto CNTs/CDs. The XPS was also performed to ascertain the synthesis of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm. As shown in Fig. 1 D, the peaks of C1s were at 283.5 eV, 284.28 eV, 287.27 eV, and 285.27 eV, which corresponded to sp2 hybridized carbon, sp3 hybridized carbon, N-C = O, and O = C-O, respectively. These analyses further demonstrated the formation of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm. The architecture type of the modifier PNIPAm has been further discussed. It has been reported that the architecture of such polymer can be divided into linear and star shaped structures, which are determined by active sites, which was AIBA on CDs [28]. To be specific, if there are more than three initiation sites on each CD, CNTs/CD-PNIPAm of star architecture can be formed through subsequent in situ polymerization reaction. Correspondingly, CNTs/CD-PNIPAm of linear architecture is more inclined to be formed while there is only one initiation site on per CD. Elemental analysis was conducted on CDs-AIBA, and the results are shown in Table S1 . After calculation, it can be determined that the molar ratio of AIBA to CDs is 3:1. That is to say, there is about 3 mol of AIBA loaded on each mole of CDs, indicating that the CNTs/CD-PNIPAm is of star architecture. The content of PNIPAm formed in the composite was obtained through thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). The amount of PNIPAm on CNTs was calculated to be approximately 73.1 wt% of the total ( Figure S4 ). The molecular weight (Mw) of PNIPAm chains in CNTs/CD-PNIPAm was characterized by GPC method. The Mw of PNIPAm chains in CNTs/CD-PNIPAm is 2851 g/mol, which is similar to the Mw of PNIPAm (2898 g/mol, Table S2 ). The polydispersity of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm is 1.02. The narrow polydispersity index indicates a uniform molecular weight distribution of PNIPAm in the composite.

The dispersion properties of CNTs/CD-PNIPAm in water were expressed using the zeta potential analysis. The zeta potential of CNTs/CDs is –23.7 mV at 20 °C ( Figure S5 ), suggesting that the CNTs/CDs are mainly stabilized by electrostatic exclusion. In order to conduct solubility experiments, each sample was dispersed at a concentration of 10 mg/mL into 1.5 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 30 min. The dispersed solution after ultrasound was left to stand at the corresponding temperature for 30 min to achieve stability. Fig. 1 E shows the changes in water dispersibility of CNTs before and after polymer modification. It can be seen that CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm form a stable dispersion in water (Fig. 1 E Ⅱ), indicating that the modification of the polymer enhanced the polarity of the hydrophobic CNTs and facilitated the formation of a homogeneous aqueous dispersion. Dispersion of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm can maintain uniform within 30 days, indicating its good storage stability ( Figure S6 ). For comparison, CNTs/PNIPAm was prepared under almost identical conditions with CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm, with the only difference being that the CDs was not introduced. On the contrary, precipitation was found in dispersion of CNTs/PNIPAm (Fig. 1 E Ⅰ). This proves that there is only insufficient interaction between CNTs and PNIPAm without CDs. Similarly, the vast majority of CNTs/CDs-PAA in solution are dispersed, while CNTs/PAA without CDs connection exist only as aggregates (Ⅲ). As evident from Fig. 1 E, the impact of CDs on linking effects is crucial for polymers to change the surface properties of CNTs. To further verify this conclusion, TEM studies were performed. As can be seen in TEM image of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm (Fig. 2
M), the layer attached on the outer surface of CNTs were actually PNIPAm which have been magnified in the inset. Once the CDs were absent, the CNTs/PNIPAm (Fiug u re 2C) formed smooth surface, with an inner diameter of about 35 nm. Similarly, PAA is attached to the surface of CNTs/CDs owing to linkage of CDs (Fig. 2 N), while such modification could not be achieved in the CNTs/PAA system (Fig. 2 D).

The stimuli-responsiveness of four distinct mixtures was individually investigated and compared. The first type of mixture comprises polymers and CNTs, namely CNTs/PNIPAm and CNTs/PAA. These mixtures were prepared through the polymerization of polymer monomers in the presence of CNTs. Specifically, CNTs/PNIPAm and CNTs/PAA were synthesized by free radical polymerization of N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAm) and acrylic acid (AA), respectively. In these reactions, CNTs were dispersed in the polymerization mixture, and 2,2′-azobis(2-methylpropionamidine) dihydrochloride (AIBA) was used as the initiator. The reaction conditions were similar to those described for the synthesis of the CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm and CNTs/CDs-PAA, the only difference is that there is no modification of CDs on CNTs. CDs were not added during the preparation of CNTs/PNIPAm and CNTs/PAA, implying the absence of a covalent bond connection between CNTs and PNIPAm or PAA. The water dispersibility of CNTs/PNIPAm and CNTs/PAA at 20 °C and 40 °C was illustrated in Fig. 2 A and B. It is evident that precipitation exists in water at 20 °C. Due to the good hydrophilicity of PNIPAm at 20° C, it can be determined that the precipitation is CNTs that have not combined with PNIPAm, indicating unsuccessful modification of PNIPAm on CNTs. As the temperature increases to 40 °C, precipitation persists in the aqueous dispersion of CNTs/PNIPAm, demonstrating that the complex did not switch between hydrophilic and hydrophobic states (Fig. 2 B). Similar observations were made in the aqueous dispersion of CNTs/PAA at different pH values (Fig. 2 E, F). These results suggest that without the involvement of CDs, the regulation of stimuli-responsive polymers on the hydrophilic-hydrophobic properties of CNTs could not be achieved.

The second type of mixture involves the blending of CNTs/CDs and PNIPAm. CDs were initially combined with CNTs through π-π interaction, and then CNTs/CDs were mixed with free PNIPAm to create the mixture. The responsiveness to stimuli of such mixture was explored to investigate the potential of free PNIPAm binding to CNTs/CDs and regulating its hydrophilic properties. Digital photos of CNTs/CDs blending with PNIPAm at different temperatures are presented in Fig. 2 G and H. It can be observed that the dispersion forms a uniform black appearance (Fig. 2 G). This is attributed to the significant improvement in hydrophilicity of CNTs’ surface achieved through the binding of CDs, enabling stable dispersion in water. Simultaneously, PNIPAm in the blend system exhibits remarkable hydrophilic properties. It is worth noting that when the temperature is increased to 40 °C, the CNTs/CDs and PNIPAm blend forms a gray dispersion, resulting from the ‘coil-to-globule’ transition of PNIPAm (Fig. 2 H). CNTs/CDs still remain uniformly dispersed in water and do not exhibit a switch between hydrophobic and hydrophilic states with temperature. This further confirms that stable integration between PNIPAm and CNTs/CDs cannot be achieved through simple blending.

The third type of mixture consists of CNTs and CDs-PNIPAm. Before mixing, NIPAm was first in-situ polymerized on CDs to form CDs-PNIPAm through a method similar to the synthesis of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm. This modification endowed PNIPAm with the potential to bind with CNTs. To achieve the blending of CNTs and CDs-PNIPAm, the mixture was sonicated for 3 h and stirred for an additional 3 h, repeated for three times. The assembly of CNTs and CDs-PNIPAm was evaluated by FT-IR before discussing its stimuli-responsiveness. It can be observed in Figure S7 that the curve of CNTs after the assembly process with CDs-PNIPAm (curve b) is consistent with that of pure CNTs (curve a). There is no characteristic peak belonging to C = O stretching vibration of secondary amide in PNIPAm appeared, proving that CDs-PNIPAm can not be combined with CNTs successfully by simply blending. We believe that the reason lies in the fact that combining CDs with AIBA at first causes the PNIPAm chains formed through NIPAm polymerization to entangle on the surface of CDs. Consequently, this hinders the π-π interaction between CDs and CNTs. The stimuli-responsive properties of CNTs and CDs-PNIPAm blends were further investigated (Fig. 2 I and J). It can be found that black precipitates at the bottom of the bottle in the aqueous dispersion of CNTs and CDs-PNIPAm blends at 20 °C (Fig. 2 I), indicating that CNTs exhibit significant hydrophobicity. This proves that CDs-PNIPAm did not effectively modify the surface of CNTs. When the temperature rises to 40 °C, the aqueous dispersion of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm (Fig. 2 J) shows a clear milky white color, demonstrating that CNTs still remain hydrophobic in the form of precipitates while the polymer chains in CDs-PNIPAm curl. That is to say, changes in the hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity of CDs-PNIPAm cannot affect CNTs. These phenomena support the conclusion that simple blending of CNTs and CDs-PNIPAm cannot lead to effective assembly.

The fourth type of mixture, CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm, and CNTs/CDs-PAA, were formed through a sequential assembly method, involving the in-situ polymerization of polymers after the combination of CNTs and CDs. The stimuli-responsive properties of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm and CNTs/CDs-PAA was shown in Fig. 2 K and L. At 20 °C, PNIPAm stretch into the solution to stabilize CNTs, forming a uniform black dispersion (Fig. 2 K). When the temperature increased to 40 °C, without any other physical interaction, PNIPAm spontaneously show a ‘coil-to-globule’ transition, making it difficult to interact with water molecules, so that CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm existing in the form of precipitation (Fig. 2 L). When it comes to CNTs/CDs-PAA, it can also switch between hydrophilic and hydrophobic based on pH stimulation (Fig. 2 O, P). It is obvious that large differences exist between the stimuli-responsiveness of these mixtures. Considering these distinctions, a clear conclusion can be drawn that only sequential assembly, comprising the modification of CDs on CNTs followed by the in-situ polymerization of polymer monomers, can achieve the preparation of stimuli-responsive CNTs with adjustable hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties.

The modulation of hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity in assembled CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm and CNTs/CDs-PAA, driven by temperature or pH stimuli, can be further investigated using water contact angles (WCAs). As depicted in Fig. 3
A and B, with an increase in temperature, the hydrophilicity of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm decreases, accompanied by a rise in hydrophobicity, leading to an elevation in WCAs from 51.3° to 91.6°. This indicated that the modified polymer tuned the surface wettability of CNTs in water. The reversibility of such regulation is investigated (Fig. 3 C). After several repeated temperature change cycles, the variation of WCAs is reversible, indicating good reversibility of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm. Similarly, CNTs/CDs-PAA also shows reversible WCAs changes in the range of 71.8° to 21.5° with the change of pH ( Fig. 3 D, E, F). The WCAs of CNTs at different temperatures and pH were shown in Figure S8 , revealing that untreated CNTs did not exhibit significant variations in contact angle under different temperature and pH conditions. From this, we can infer that the changes in WCAs for CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm and CNTs/CDs-PAA are attributed to the modifications made by PNIPAm and PAA. With the assistance of CDs, the polymers encapsulate the CNT surface. When the polymer chains are extended, the composite material exhibits the hydrophilic properties of the polymers. As the temperature increases or the pH decreases, the polymer chains contract, exposing the hydrophobic surface of the CNTs. Consequently, this controlled alteration in contact angle of the modified CNTs can be achieved through temperature or pH regulation. These results support that CDs can provide reliable connections between stimuli-responsive polymers and CNTs during conformation changes of polymers, which provides a solid foundation for further application of stimuli-responsive CNTs in electrochemical devices.

The conversion of hydrophobic and hydrophilic properties can realize the construction of electrochemical switches, such as the electrochemical switching ability of hydrophilic probes. Cyclic voltammograms of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm/GC modified electrodes were obtained at 20 °C and 40 °C. Fig. 4
A shows the schematic illustration of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm/GC modified electrode. The PNIPAm chain stretched to reveal the CNT, which provided pathways for the electron transfer. As can be seen from Fig. 4 B, CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm/GC modified electrode exhibited a pair of reversible redox peaks at 20 °C, which belongs to the Fe(CN)6 3−/ Fe(CN)6 4− redox pair, demonstrating excellent electron transport properties [31]. The specific electrode redox reaction was as follows:
(1)
Fe
CN
6
3 -
+
e
-
↔
Fe
CN
6
4 -

The introduction of CNTs has significantly improved the electric transfer capability of stimuli-responsive polymers. It means that the electrochemical response is in the ‘ON’ state at 20 °C. The redox peaks disappeared as the temperature was elevated to 40 °C. Such change of CV signals was attributed to the collapse of PNIPAm chain upon temperature variation, which sealed the CNTs to hinder the electron transfer. That is to say, the electrochemical response is in the ‘OFF’ state at 40 °C. The reversibility of this regulation is further studied. It can be seen from Fig. 4 C that after three temperature change cycles, the peak current of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm/GC electrode retains 92 % of the original signal, showing good reversibility. Table S3 shows the comparison of electrochemical performance and cycle numbers of different modified electrodes. The interfacial electrode transfer resistance (Ret) also illustrates this phenomenon ( Figure S9 ). Figure S9
A shows the EIS spectra of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm/GC modified electrodes in a supporting electrolyte solution of 5 mM K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 (1:1) and 0.5 M KCl, at temperatures of 20 °C and 40 °C. By fitting equivalent circuits, the electrode Ret was calculated as 5888 Ω at 20 °C. With an increase in temperature, the resistance of the CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm/GC modified electrode increased to 10090 Ω at 40 °C, which is 1.71 times higher than the original value. This is attributed to the contraction of PNIPAm chains, hindering the access of the Fe(CN)6 3-/4- probe to the electrode surface, thus entering into the ‘off’ state of the intelligent response switch. Similarly, as shown in Figure S9
C, with a change in solution pH from 3 to 10, the Ret of the CNTs/CDs-PAA/GC modified electrode increased from 113 Ω to 789 Ω, an increase of 6.98 times. This conclusion demonstrates that polymer chains can modulate the electron transfer capability of the modified electrode through conformational changes, laying the foundation for the application of electrode materials in switchable sensors.

A simple and non-destructive method to synthesis different types of stimuli-responsive polymers on CNTs by using carbonous materials CDs as internal linker was constructed. Benefiting from the connection of CDs, PNIPAm and PAA were successfully modified on the CNTs. The as prepared CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm and CNTs/CDs-PAA exhibit brilliant 'ON-OFF' switching responsiveness on surface hydrophilicity and electrical activity against temperature or pH. The polymer chains on the prepared modified electrode are stimulated to regulate stretching or curling, allowing or hindering the entry of Fe(CN)6 3-/4- probes into the electrode surface. This responsive behavior manifests a reversible ‘ON-OFF’ switching effect in response to alterations in temperature or pH. Compared with traditional methods, this method avoids destructive pre-treatment of CNTs. Due to the non-destructive characteristics and directional modification ability, the inherent electronic properties of CNTs have been well maintained to express better performance in electrochemical detection. These stimuli-responsive CNTs hold the promise of being employed in the construction of switchable electrochemical sensors and devices.

TEM images of CDs, CNTs, CNTs/CDs; Zeta potential of CNTs, CNTs/CDs; FT-IR spectra of CDs, CDs-AIBA, CNTs, CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm; TGA curves for CNTs, CNTs/CDs, CDs, CNTs/CDs-PAA, CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm, PAA and PNIPAm; Digital image of CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm, CNTs/CDs-PAA; Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) for PNIPAm/GCE, PAA/GCE; Water contact angles of CNTs; The reduction peak current of cyclic voltammograms of PNIPAm/GCE, CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm/GCE, PAA/GCE, CNTs/CDs-PAA/GCE; Elemental analysis results of CDs-AIBA; GPC analysis results of PNIPAm, CNTs/CDs-PNIPAm; Comparison of electrochemical performance and cycle numbers of different modified electrodes.
This work is supported by the Liaoning Revitalization Talents Program of China (XLYC2002097) and the Key Projects of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (JYTZD2023001).

Huiting Wang: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Xiaoyan Xie: Investigation, Data curation. Dongxue Lin: Investigation. Jie Zhou: Investigation. Ling Zhang: Supervision, Methodology. Zhiqiang Xing: Supervision. Qian Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Conceptualization. Lixin Xia: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of multifunctional electrochemical sensor based on electroactivity-adjustable poly (ionic liquids)/reduced graphene oxide
